title: What Is LangGraph? Graph-Based Agent Orchestration
seoTitle: What Is LangGraph? Graph Orchestration for Agents
metaDescription: LangGraph is a low-level framework for building stateful AI agents as graphs, with explicit control over loops, branching, and persistence.
oneLiner: You will understand how LangGraph models an agent as a graph of nodes and edges, giving you explicit control over state, loops, and branching that a simple agent loop hides.

blocks.md: Most beginner [AI agents](/learn/ai-agents/agent-fundamentals/what-is-an-ai-agent) run as a simple `while` loop: call the model, run whatever [tool](/learn/ai-agents/tool-use/what-is-tool-use) it asked for, feed the result back, repeat until it stops. That loop is fine for a demo, but it hides everything. You can't easily pause it, can't branch it down two different paths, can't replay it after a crash, and can't see *why* it took the step it did. The control flow lives invisibly inside one function.

**LangGraph** turns that hidden loop into a thing you can see and shape: a **graph**. You describe your agent as a set of **nodes** (steps that do work) connected by **edges** (rules for what runs next), and they all read from and write to one shared **state** object. Instead of control flow being trapped inside a loop, it becomes an explicit map you draw.

Think of it like a board game. The **state** is the board — the current position everyone can see. Each **node** is a square that does something when you land on it ("draw a card", "roll again"). The **edges** are the arrows that say where you go next, and some of those arrows are conditional ("if you rolled a six, take this path"). A plain agent loop is a game with one square and a single arrow looping back on itself. LangGraph lets you draw the whole board, including forks, loops, and a square marked *pause here and ask a human*.

LangGraph comes from the team behind [LangChain](/learn/agent-frameworks/langchain-ecosystem/what-is-langchain), but it is a separate, **lower-level** library. You can use it without using the rest of LangChain. Its job is orchestration — deciding what runs when — not wrapping models or prompts.
The moment an agent leaves the demo stage and has to be reliable, the invisible loop becomes a liability. LangGraph exists because real agents need four things that a bare loop makes painful.

items: **Cycles you control.** Real agents retry, reflect, and re-plan — they loop. A graph makes that loop explicit, with a clear condition for when to keep going and when to stop, so you never ship an agent that spins forever or quits one step too early.

**Branching.** "If the request is a refund, go down this path; if it's a complaint, go down that one." Conditional edges let one model decision route the whole workflow, instead of you nesting if-statements around an opaque loop.

**Persistence and recovery.** Because the entire agent's progress lives in one state object, LangGraph can save a snapshot after every step. If the process crashes — or the user comes back tomorrow — you resume exactly where you left off instead of starting over.

**Human-in-the-loop.** Some actions (refunding money, sending an email, deleting a record) should pause for a person to approve. A graph can stop at a node, wait — for seconds or for days — and continue when the human responds, because the saved state is just sitting there.

blocks.md: The deeper payoff is **debuggability**. When an agent misbehaves, "it's somewhere in the loop" is not a useful answer. With a graph you can see exactly which node ran, what the state looked like before and after, and which edge it took. That visibility is the difference between an agent you *hope* works and one you can actually operate in production.

Who should care? Anyone building an agent that does more than one straight-line task: support bots that escalate, research agents that loop until they have enough sources, multi-step workflows that need approval gates, or any system where a failure halfway through must be recoverable rather than catastrophic.

LangGraph has exactly three core ideas. Once they click, the whole framework follows: **state** (the shared data), **nodes** (functions that update it), and **edges** (rules that decide the next node).

blocks.text: State: the single source of truth

blocks.md: You define a state schema — usually a typed dictionary listing the fields your agent tracks, like the message history, a counter, or a draft answer. Every node receives the current state and returns an update to it. Nothing important lives in hidden local variables; if a step needs to remember something, it writes it into state. This is what makes pausing, saving, and resuming possible — the agent's *entire* memory is one inspectable object.

blocks.text: Nodes: the steps that do work

blocks.md: A node is just a function. One node might call the model, another might run a tool, another might validate output or format a final answer. A node reads the state, does its job, and returns the fields it wants to change. LangGraph merges that update back into the shared state and moves on.

blocks.text: Edges: the rules for what runs next

blocks.md: Edges connect nodes. A **normal edge** always goes from A to B. A **conditional edge** runs a small function that looks at the current state and *picks* the next node — this is how you get branching and loops. To loop, you simply draw an edge from a later node back to an earlier one; to branch, your condition returns different destinations. A special `END` marker tells the graph to stop.

diagram.title: A tool-using agent as a graph

steps.label: tools node
steps.sub: run tool, write result to state

blocks.md: The loop is the key part of that picture: after the **tools node** runs, an edge sends control *back* to the **agent node**, which now sees the tool result in state and decides again — call another tool, or finish. The conditional edge after the agent node is the brain of the loop. It inspects the model's last message and routes to `tools` if the model asked for a tool, or to `END` if it produced a final answer. That single decision point is the classic agent loop, but now it is a visible, editable edge instead of buried code.

blocks.title: the shape of a LangGraph agent (sketch)
blocks.code:
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END
from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
from operator import add

# 1) STATE: one shared object every node reads and writes.
class State(TypedDict):
    messages: Annotated[list, add]   # new messages are appended, not replaced

# 2) NODES: plain functions that return a state update.
def agent(state: State):
    reply = call_model(state["messages"])     # your model call
    return {"messages": [reply]}

def tools(state: State):
    result = run_tool(state["messages"][-1])  # execute the requested tool
    return {"messages": [result]}

# 3) EDGES: a condition decides the next node -> this creates the loop.
def route(state: State):
    last = state["messages"][-1]
    return "tools" if last.wants_tool else END

graph = StateGraph(State)
graph.add_node("agent", agent)
graph.add_node("tools", tools)
graph.add_edge(START, "agent")
graph.add_conditional_edges("agent", route)  # branch: tools or END
graph.add_edge("tools", "agent")             # loop back to the agent

app = graph.compile()

blocks.md: Notice the `Annotated[list, add]` on `messages`. That tells LangGraph *how* to merge updates to that field — here, append new messages instead of overwriting them. These merge rules (called reducers) are what let many nodes safely contribute to the same state, even when they run in parallel.

This is where the graph model earns its keep over a plain loop. When you compile a graph with a **checkpointer**, LangGraph saves a snapshot of the full state after every step, keyed by a thread id (one conversation, one task). Three powerful behaviors fall out of that almost for free.

layers.label: Resume after a crash
layers.sub: reload the last checkpoint and continue, no lost progress

layers.label: Pause for human approval
layers.sub: stop at a node, wait minutes or days, then resume

layers.label: Time travel
layers.sub: rewind to an earlier checkpoint and try a different path

layers.label: Memory across sessions
layers.sub: the same thread id picks up yesterday's conversation

blocks.md: **Human-in-the-loop** is the headline use case. You mark a point in the graph as an interrupt — say, right before a node that spends money. The graph runs up to there, stops, and returns control to your application. The state is safely checkpointed. A human reviews it, approves or edits, and you call the graph again with the same thread id to continue from that exact spot. Nothing re-runs, nothing is lost, and the wait can be arbitrarily long because the agent's progress is just a saved record, not a live process holding memory.

In development the checkpointer can be in-memory; in production you point it at a real database so state survives restarts and scales across many concurrent threads. Your graph code does not change — only where the snapshots are stored.

The most common confusion is **LangGraph versus LangChain**, and the difference is the level of control. LangChain gives you building blocks (model wrappers, prompt templates, tool definitions) and some prebuilt chains. LangGraph gives you the *orchestration layer* — the explicit graph that decides how those pieces connect, loop, and branch. They are complementary: you can use LangChain components as the work *inside* LangGraph nodes.

The other axis is **control versus convenience**. Higher-level, role-based frameworks like [CrewAI](/learn/agent-frameworks/orchestration-frameworks/what-is-crewai) or [AutoGen](/learn/agent-frameworks/orchestration-frameworks/what-is-autogen) hand you opinionated abstractions — "agents", "roles", "conversations" — that get a multi-agent demo running in a few lines. LangGraph deliberately stays lower-level: you wire the control flow by hand. That is more work up front, and far more control when the easy path stops fitting your problem.

A useful rule of thumb: reach for an opinionated framework when your problem fits its shape, and reach for LangGraph when you need to define the shape yourself. As of today it is the recognized category leader for production-grade agent orchestration precisely because it refuses to hide the control flow from you.
LangGraph is powerful, and most early mistakes come from fighting its model instead of leaning into it.

items: **Treating it like a chain.** If your task is a fixed, straight line of steps with no loops or branches, a graph is overkill — a simple sequence is clearer. Use LangGraph when control flow is actually dynamic.

**Hiding state in globals.** The instant a node stashes something in a module-level variable instead of the state object, persistence and resume break — that data won't be in the checkpoint. Everything the agent needs to remember must live in state.

**Forgetting reducers.** If two nodes write the same field and you didn't define a merge rule, one update silently overwrites the other. For accumulating fields (like message history) you almost always want an append reducer.

**Unbounded loops.** A conditional edge with a condition that never returns `END` will loop until you hit a recursion limit. Always give every loop a concrete exit — a counter, a confidence check, or a max-steps guard.

**Skipping the checkpointer in production.** Compiling without a durable checkpointer throws away the framework's biggest advantage. Wire in a real persistence backend before you ship anything that must survive a restart.

blocks.md: A graph does not make a bad agent good. If your prompts are weak or your tools are unreliable, LangGraph will faithfully orchestrate a bad agent. It controls *flow*, not *quality* — you still need good prompts, good tools, and [evaluation](/learn/ai-agents/agent-fundamentals/what-is-an-ai-agent).
The node/edge/state model scales up in a few directions worth knowing once the basics click.

**Multi-agent as subgraphs.** A node doesn't have to be a single function — it can be an entire graph. That lets you build a "supervisor" graph whose nodes are themselves specialist agents (a researcher subgraph, a writer subgraph), with the supervisor's edges routing work between them. Multi-agent systems become graphs of graphs, all sharing the same state and persistence machinery rather than each reinventing it.

**Parallel branches and fan-out.** Conditional edges can dispatch to *several* nodes at once, run them concurrently, and merge their results back into shared state using reducers. This is how you parallelize independent work — say, querying three sources at the same time — without hand-managing threads. The reducer you attached to each field decides how the parallel updates combine.

**Streaming and observability.** Because every step is an explicit node, LangGraph can stream events as the graph runs — node started, state updated, token produced — which is what powers live "thinking" UIs and step-by-step traces. Pairing it with a tracing tool like [LangSmith](/learn/agent-frameworks/langchain-ecosystem/what-is-langchain) gives you a recorded timeline of every node and state transition for debugging.

**Durable execution.** The combination of checkpointing and explicit steps means a long-running agent can be treated like a workflow engine: it can sleep, wait on external events, and survive deploys mid-run. This is the bridge from "chatbot that answers in one pass" to "agent that works on a task over hours with humans looping in."

Where to go next: study how it sits in the broader LangChain ecosystem in [what is LangChain](/learn/agent-frameworks/langchain-ecosystem/what-is-langchain), compare orchestration philosophies against [LlamaIndex](/learn/agent-frameworks/orchestration-frameworks/what-is-llamaindex) and role-based [CrewAI](/learn/agent-frameworks/orchestration-frameworks/what-is-crewai), then read the official docs to see the exact APIs for state schemas, conditional edges, and checkpointers. The durable lesson is simple: by making control flow a visible graph instead of a hidden loop, LangGraph trades a little upfront effort for agents you can actually inspect, recover, and trust.

faq.a: LangGraph is used to build stateful AI agents and multi-step workflows as graphs of nodes and edges. It is the right tool when an agent needs loops, branching, the ability to pause for human approval, or recovery after a crash — anything beyond a simple straight-line call to a model.

faq.q: What is the difference between LangChain and LangGraph?
faq.a: LangChain provides building blocks like model wrappers, prompt templates, and tool definitions. LangGraph is a separate, lower-level orchestration layer that controls *how* those pieces connect, loop, and branch via an explicit graph. They are complementary — you can run LangChain components inside LangGraph nodes — and you can use LangGraph without the rest of LangChain.

Conceptually it is close to one. You define nodes (states/steps), a shared state object, and edges (transitions, including conditional ones) that decide what runs next. The difference is that LangGraph is built specifically for LLM agents, with first-class support for loops, persistence, streaming, and human-in-the-loop pauses on top of that state-machine-like structure.

faq.q: How does LangGraph handle memory and persistence?
faq.a: All of an agent's progress lives in one shared state object. When you compile a graph with a checkpointer, LangGraph saves a snapshot of that state after every step, keyed by a thread id. That lets the same thread resume after a crash, pick up an earlier conversation, or pause for a human and continue later — without re-running completed work.

faq.q: Do I need LangGraph, or can I just write a loop?
faq.a: For a quick prototype, a plain `while` loop is fine. You want LangGraph once you need explicit branching, controlled loops, persistence and resume, human approval gates, or step-by-step debuggability — the things a hidden loop makes painful. The rule of thumb: simple straight-line tasks don't need a graph; dynamic, recoverable, production agents do.

Yes. A node can itself be an entire subgraph, so you can build a supervisor graph whose nodes are specialist agents and whose edges route work between them. Every agent shares the same state and persistence machinery, which is cleaner than wiring several independent agents together by hand.

furtherReading.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models (arXiv)
